text: Working space is created simply by deflating the left lung after the initial choke bar is placed.
A blunt retractor device can be used to provide additional exposure as needed.
The dissection of the diverticulum is begun from the distal end of the esophagus.
Initially, the inferior pulmonary ligament is divided for better exposure.
The space between the esophagus and the aorta is identified and then developed.
A penrose is encircled around the esophagus.
Once in place, the penrose can act as a handle for retraction.
Care must be taken to avoid injuring the vagus nerves.

If extra instrumentation is needed for better retraction, it may be placed anywhere in the intercostal space, through a stab wound without a formal trocar since there is no gas and sufflection to escape.
The rest of the diverticulum can be dissected using standard instruments, including electrocautery or ultrasound dissector.
Dissection is carried out well above the diverticulum as well.
Thank you.
When the retroesophageal space is developed, a second Penrose drain is placed in the superior aspects.
This will oscillate the diverticulum superiorly and inferiorly by the Penrose drains.
Eventually the diverticulum is completely and meticulously dissected out.
The diverticulum is prepared for esophagoscopy and then stapling.
Esophagoscopy is performed to ensure that all of the diverticulum is isolated, that the dilator would pass easily, and that there is no debris within the diverticulum.
After placing a 60 French bougie dilator, the diverticulum is stapled using a laparoscopic stapling device.
Ideally, the stapler should be placed parallel to the length of the esophagus.
However, due to the variability of anatomy and inherent restrictions placed by the thoracic wall, this may not be possible.
Carrier is taken to place the stapler as to avoid stricture of the esophagus.
If the diverticulum is of full thickness, a thick tissue stapling device may be needed.
A second statement cartridge is usually needed as well.
Where needed,
Individual stitches may be placed to further support the staple line.
These are tied interproporally.
Post staple esophagoscopy is performed to rule out leaks and to ensure adequate lumen of the esophagus.
Since our patient had normal distal esophagus, myotomy was not performed.
However, should a myotomy be needed, it can be performed at this time as shown in this another patient.
When completed, the esophagus is replaced to its normal position.
The nasal gastric tube is inserted for post-operative protection.
A chest tube is placed to one of the trocar sites and the lung is reinflated.
The trocar defects are closed in standard fashion.
On post-op follow-up at three weeks, our patient had healed up very well and was enjoying regular meals once again.
